title: Nematicity in LaFeAsO single crystals studied by elastoresistance, high-resolution thermal expansion and shear-modulus measurements
authors: X. C. Hong, S. Sauerland, L. Wang, F. Scaravaggi, A. U. B. Wolter, R. Kappenberger, S. Aswartham, S. Wurmehl, S. Sykora, F. Caglieris, B. Büchner, C. Hess, R. Klingeler
abstract: Nematicity in LaFeAsO single crystals is studied by means of high-resolution thermal expansion, shear modulus, and elastoresistivity measurements. A softening of the shear modulus $C_{\rm 66}$ towards the structural phase transition at $T_{\rm S}$ is observed. In addition, a similar Curie-Weiss-like divergence of the nematic susceptibilities is found in the temperature dependence of both $χ^{sh}$ and $χ^{er}$, which are deduced from the shear modulus (sh) and the elastoresistivity (er) studies, respectively. These observations provide evidence for an electronic origin of nematicity in LaFeAsO. The characteristic energy of the coupling between the lattice and the electronic degrees of freedom is deduced to $\sim$30~K. The comparison to corresponding measurements on BaFe$_2$As$_2$ single crystals reveals a very similar temperature dependence of the shear modulus but yields contrasting results for $χ^{er}$ : In BaFe$_2$As$_2$, $χ^{er}$ diverges similarly as the uncoupled nematicity deduced from the shear modulus data as it is expected from the underlying Landau theory. In contrast, the Weiss temperatures of $χ^{er}$ and $χ^{sh}$ are significantly different in LaFeAsO. This difference is at odds with the commonly anticipated theories of resistivity anisotropy and electronic nematicity in iron pnictides.
summary: This paper uses elastoresistance and elastic measurements to study the structural transition in LaFeAsO iron-based superconductors. It identifies an electronic origin for nematicity but finds that the temperature scales of different nematic susceptibilities do not match, challenging current theoretical models of resistivity anisotropy in pnictides.
main_problem: Investigating the microscopic origin of electronic nematicity in LaFeAsO and determining whether the structural transition is driven by an electronic or elastic instability.
main_result: The study finds evidence for an electronic origin of nematicity via Curie-Weiss-like divergence in susceptibilities, but reveals a significant discrepancy in LaFeAsO where the elastoresistivity Weiss temperature differs from the shear modulus Weiss temperature, contradicting standard theories.
method: High-resolution thermal expansion, shear modulus measurements via three-point bending, and elastoresistivity measurements using a piezoelectric actuator.
model_or_system: LaFeAsO single crystals (1111-type iron-based superconductors) undergoing a tetragonal-to-orthorhombic structural transition.
key_observables: Shear modulus (C66), nematic susceptibility (chi_sh and chi_er), orthorhombic distortion (delta), and thermal expansion coefficients.
important_parameters: Structural transition temperature (Ts), magnetic transition temperature (TN), and electron-lattice coupling energy scale (~30 K).
main_assumptions: A pseudo-proper ferroelastic approach using a Landau expansion of free energy with bilinear coupling between electronic order and elastic strain.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows relative length changes, orthorhombic distortion, and thermal expansion coefficients; Figure A1 shows normalized Young's modulus scaling between LaFeAsO and BaFe2As2.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nematicity in 1111-type superconductors, details the experimental setup for thermal expansion and elastoresistance, presents the observed softening of C66 and divergence of susceptibilities, compares these results to the 122-type BaFe2As2 system, and discusses the implications for existing theories of resistivity anisotropy.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Relative length changes ∆L/L of the three main crystallographic axes (left scale) and orthorhombic distortion δ = (a −b)/(a + b) (right scale) vs. temperature. Structural and magnetic phase transition temperatures, TS and TN, of this crystal are marked by dashed lines. (b) Corresponding uniaxial thermal expansion coefficients. (c) Young’s modulus Y[110] along the [110]t direction (left scale) and bare nematic susceptibility λ2χsh/C66,0 (right scale) together with Curie-Weiss fittings (dashed lines) according to Eq. 7. The inset shows a schematic sketch of the utilized 3PB-setup where the sample bends under an applied force (blue arrow).

FIG. 2. Temperature dependence of the nematic susceptibility measured by elastroresistance χer = −dη/dϵ. χer in the B2g- and the B1g-symmetry channels was investigated by applying strain ϵ along the [110]t and [100]t crystal directions, respectively. Dashed lines indicate TS and TN. The blue dashed line shows Curie-Weiss-fitting according to Eq. 8. The inset shows a photograph of a crystal glued on the surface of a PZT stack.

FIG. 3. Temperature dependence of the inverse purely electronic nematic susceptibilities (λ2χsh/C66,0)−1 (left scale, green markers) and (χer−χer 0 )−1 (right scale, blue markers) of (a) BaFe2As2 and (b) LaFeAsO obtained by shear modulus and elastoresistivity measurements, respectively. χer 0 accounts for the intrinsic elastoresistivity. In addition, (λ2eχsh/C66,0)−1 (left scale, red markers) with the actual (renormalized) nematic susceptibility eχsh is shown. The dashed lines represent Curie-Weiss fits to the data. Characteristic temperatures are indicated by grey markers (see the text).
